days.title: Inheritance and Polymorphism
learningObjectives: Explain Inheritance and Polymorphism with clear interview-ready wording
Implement Inheritance and Polymorphism using compilable Java examples for Day 10
Identify and prevent common bugs related to Inheritance and Polymorphism
Discuss design and performance trade-offs for Inheritance and Polymorphism
Solve at least one realistic exercise based on Inheritance and Polymorphism
Answer conceptual, coding, and scenario interview questions confidently

sections.title: Why Inheritance and Polymorphism matters
sections.content:
Inheritance and Polymorphism is a high-impact topic for Day 10. It directly supports OOP principles and core Java APIs and appears in interviews as a depth-check area.

Why this matters in practice:
- Teams rely on this concept for clean implementation decisions.
- Mistakes here create maintainability and production-risk issues.
- Strong understanding improves both coding rounds and system discussions.

Learning strategy for this day:
1. Define the concept clearly in one line.
2. Implement one minimal Java example.
3. Validate at least one edge/failure case.
4. Explain one trade-off (simplicity vs flexibility, speed vs safety, etc.).
5. Rephrase your answer as if explaining to an interviewer.

If you can explain Inheritance and Polymorphism clearly and implement it without copy-paste, you are interview-ready for this unit.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Inheritance and Polymorphism
sections.content:
### Day 10 Topic: Inheritance and Polymorphism
This lesson belongs to Phase 2 (OOP principles and core Java APIs) and targets Beginner level expectations.

### Core Concept
Start by defining Inheritance and Polymorphism in precise terms, then connect it to runtime behavior and design impact.

### Internal Understanding
For this topic, interviewers expect more than API recall. They look for:
- correct mental model
- edge-case awareness
- clean implementation choices
- trade-off reasoning

### Practical Implementation Approach
1. Build a baseline solution that is correct and readable.
2. Add validation for boundary, null/empty, or failure scenarios.
3. Measure or reason about time/space or operational cost.
4. Refactor into maintainable units.

### Interview Structure (recommended)
- What is it?
- Why is it needed?
- One practical Java example
- One common pitfall
- One better alternative (if constraints change)

### Production Perspective
In real systems, correctness alone is not enough. Inheritance and Polymorphism should also support observability, safe changes, and team readability.

### Revision Checklist
- Can you explain this without notes?
- Can you code it from memory?
- Can you test one edge case quickly?
- Can you justify your approach in 30-60 seconds?

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Subclass extends superclass: inherits fields and can override methods.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Runtime polymorphism: method selection uses the object’s actual class (Dog), not the variable type.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Abstract method forces subclasses to implement; base class defines shared algorithm skeleton.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 10
sections.description: Visual model for Inheritance and Polymorphism

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Treating Inheritance and Polymorphism as memorization instead of implementation practice.
Skipping edge-case tests and validating only happy path.
Using unclear naming and tightly coupled method logic.
Giving interview answers without concrete examples.
Ignoring trade-offs while choosing approach.

sections.title: Exercise - Practice Inheritance and Polymorphism
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution for **Inheritance and Polymorphism** with practical constraints.

Requirements:
1. Handle at least 5 test inputs.
2. Include one explicit edge/failure case.
3. Print deterministic output for verification.
4. Add short comments for decision points.
5. Keep the solution interview-readable and maintainable.
hints: Start with correctness first, optimize later.
Add one guard clause for invalid input.
Refactor method names before final answer.
sections.solution:
// Day 10 Inheritance and Polymorphism - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is inheritance in Java?
conceptual.answer: Subclass extends superclass, inheriting fields and methods (subject to access rules).

conceptual.question: What is polymorphism?
conceptual.answer: A superclass reference can point to subclass objects; overridden methods are invoked dynamically.

conceptual.question: What is dynamic method dispatch?
conceptual.answer: JVM selects implementation based on actual object type at runtime for instance methods.

conceptual.question: Can constructors be inherited?
conceptual.answer: No—subclasses call super(...) but do not inherit superclass constructors as their own.

conceptual.question: What is @Override for?
conceptual.answer: Compiler checks that you truly override an instance method—catches signature mistakes.

conceptual.question: What is hiding of static methods?
conceptual.answer: Subclass defines static with same name—compile-time binding, not polymorphic.

conceptual.question: What is covariant return in override?
conceptual.answer: Subclass may return narrower type than superclass method.

conceptual.question: Why avoid deep inheritance trees?
conceptual.answer: Hard to reason, fragile base class; composition often clearer.

conceptual.question: What is abstract class vs concrete?
conceptual.answer: Abstract cannot be instantiated; may contain abstract and concrete methods.

conceptual.question: What is Object.equals contract?
conceptual.answer: Reflexive, symmetric, transitive, consistent; with hashCode consistency.

conceptual.question: What is Liskov Substitution Principle?
conceptual.answer: Subtypes must be substitutable for base types without breaking expectations.

conceptual.question: What happens if subclass constructor omits super()?
conceptual.answer: Compiler inserts implicit super() if no args—fails if no matching superclass constructor.

conceptual.question: Can you make private method “override”?
conceptual.answer: No—private methods are not inherited; subclass method is unrelated.

conceptual.question: What is final class impact on inheritance?
conceptual.answer: Cannot subclass—useful for security and immutability guarantees.

conceptual.question: What is instanceof with sealed hierarchy?
conceptual.answer: Exhaustive switches possible with compiler help.

codeBased.question: Abstract Animal with abstract speak; Dog implements.
codeBased.answer: abstract class Animal { abstract void speak(); }

codeBased.question: Demonstrate wrong overload vs override.
codeBased.answer: Same name different params is overload, not override.

codeBased.question: Polymorphic collection List<Animal> with Dog instances.
Use template method pattern with final execute.

seniorScenario.question: Hibernate proxy breaks equals with inheritance—issue?
seniorScenario.answer: Class identity vs instance; use business keys or composition.

seniorScenario.question: JSON polymorphic deserialization—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Open type gadgets; use allowlists, @JsonTypeInfo carefully.

seniorScenario.question: Breaking API: making method final in base—impact?
seniorScenario.answer: Subclasses can no longer override—major version bump.

seniorScenario.question: Inheritance for code reuse only—smell?
seniorScenario.answer: Often composition + delegation clearer than “is-a” lie.

seniorScenario.question: Testing abstract class behavior?
seniorScenario.answer: Concrete test subclass or Mockito spy partial mocks.

seniorScenario.question: Diamond problem in Java?
seniorScenario.answer: Single inheritance for classes; interfaces with default methods need explicit resolution rules.

wrongAnswers: static methods participate in dynamic dispatch like instance methods.
You can override private methods in subclasses.
Constructors are inherited from the parent class.
Polymorphism means the variable type decides which method runs.
final class can still be extended by nested subclasses arbitrarily.
equals can be symmetric even if subclasses add fields without changing contract.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define Inheritance and Polymorphism in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 2 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |

sections.description: interface defines contract; class implements all abstract methods.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Abstract class can hold state; interface cannot (before Java 8) — mix behavior + shared fields.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern

// Day 11 Abstract Classes and Interfaces - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is an interface in Java?
conceptual.answer: A contract of abstract methods a class implements; Java 8+ allows default and static methods.

conceptual.question: What is an abstract class?
conceptual.answer: May have state, constructors, and mix abstract/concrete methods—cannot be instantiated.

conceptual.question: When choose interface vs abstract class?
conceptual.answer: Interface for capability across unrelated types; abstract class for shared implementation and state.

conceptual.question: What is a functional interface?
conceptual.answer: Exactly one abstract method—target for lambdas (@FunctionalInterface).

conceptual.question: What is multiple interface implementation?
conceptual.answer: A class can implement many interfaces; only single inheritance for classes.

conceptual.question: What is default method conflict?
conceptual.answer: If two interfaces provide default with same signature, class must override and choose.

conceptual.question: What is private method in interface (Java 9+)?
conceptual.answer: Helper for default methods, not part of API surface.

conceptual.question: What is sealed interface (Java 17+)?
conceptual.answer: Restricts which types can implement—exhaustive switches.

conceptual.question: What is marker interface?
conceptual.answer: No methods—Serializable, Cloneable signal capability (legacy pattern).

conceptual.question: What is Comparable vs Comparator?
conceptual.answer: Natural ordering inside class vs external ordering strategy.

conceptual.question: What is interface inheritance?
conceptual.answer: Interface extends other interfaces, inheriting abstract and default methods.

conceptual.question: Why prefer interfaces for dependencies in DI?
conceptual.answer: Easier testing with mocks and swapping implementations.

conceptual.question: What is abstract method in interface before Java 8?
conceptual.answer: Implicitly public abstract; still true for non-default methods.

conceptual.question: Can interface have static fields?
conceptual.answer: Yes—public static final implicitly for fields.

conceptual.question: What is evolution of interface with default methods?
conceptual.answer: Add new behavior without breaking existing implementers—careful with semantics.

codeBased.question: interface Drawable { void draw(); }
codeBased.answer: class Circle implements Drawable { public void draw(){} }

codeBased.question: static method in interface.
codeBased.answer: static Utils parse(){ return new Utils(); }

codeBased.question: Abstract class Shape with field color.
Private method used by defaults (Java 9+).
codeBased.answer: private void helper(){} default void x(){ helper(); }

codeBased.question: Implement two interfaces with same default—resolve.
Comparable<String> natural order.
codeBased.answer: class X implements Comparable<X>{ public int compareTo(X o){...}}

codeBased.question: sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rect

seniorScenario.question: Library adds default method—binary compatibility?
seniorScenario.answer: Existing implementers get default at runtime; watch behavioral changes.

seniorScenario.question: God interface with 40 methods—fix?
seniorScenario.answer: Split into cohesive interfaces (Interface Segregation Principle).

seniorScenario.question: Testing default method behavior?
seniorScenario.answer: Concrete stub class or mock implements interface.

seniorScenario.question: Framework requires no-interface bean—workaround?
seniorScenario.answer: Use concrete with interface extracted in your code at boundaries.

seniorScenario.question: Migration from abstract class to interface—strategy?
seniorScenario.answer: Introduce interface, delegate, deprecate abstract over releases.

seniorScenario.question: Default method in JDK used in lambda-heavy hot path—concern?
seniorScenario.answer: Megamorphic call sites; profile before micro-optimizing.

wrongAnswers: A class can extend multiple classes in Java.
interface methods can be private abstract.
default methods must be overridden by implementing classes.
Abstract classes cannot have constructors.
Functional interfaces can have two abstract methods if one is default.
Marker interfaces are deprecated and unused in modern Java.

sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define Abstract Classes and Interfaces in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 2 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Private fields + getters: hide internal state (encapsulation).

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Defensive copy: return a copy of internal array so callers cannot mutate your state.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Immutable object: all fields final, no setters — thread-safe sharing pattern.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 12
sections.description: Visual model for Encapsulation and Immutability

Ignoring trade-offs while choosing approach.

// Day 12 Encapsulation and Immutability - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is encapsulation?
conceptual.answer: Bundling data with behavior and hiding internal representation behind accessors.

conceptual.question: What is immutability?
conceptual.answer: Object state cannot change after construction—simplifies reasoning and concurrency.

conceptual.question: How to make a class immutable?
conceptual.answer: final class, final fields, no setters, defensive copies in getters, no leaking mutable internals.

conceptual.question: Why defensive copy for getters returning collections?
conceptual.answer: Callers could modify internal list if you return reference.

conceptual.question: What is JavaBeans naming?
conceptual.answer: getX/setX conventions for tooling—may break immutability if setters added carelessly.

conceptual.question: What is record immutability (Java 16+)?
conceptual.answer: Shallow immutability: components final, accessors, equals/hashCode/toString generated.

conceptual.question: What is builder pattern for?
conceptual.answer: Construct complex immutable objects stepwise with validation at build().

conceptual.question: What is validation in setter?
conceptual.answer: Reject invalid state early—preserve class invariants.

conceptual.question: Why avoid exposing mutable Date from getters?
conceptual.answer: Caller can mutate; return Instant or immutable type or copy.

conceptual.question: What is unmodifiable vs immutable?
conceptual.answer: Unmodifiable view prevents modification through that reference; underlying may still change elsewhere.

conceptual.question: What is copy constructor?
conceptual.answer: New instance from existing, copying fields—clone alternative.

conceptual.question: What is String immutability benefit?
conceptual.answer: Safe sharing, caching in pool, hashCode cacheable.

conceptual.question: What is volatile for visibility?
conceptual.answer: Reads/writes go to main memory—related to concurrency, not full mutex.

conceptual.question: What is AtomicReference for immutable swaps?
conceptual.answer: Atomic updates to reference pointing to immutable objects.

conceptual.question: Why prefer final local variables?
conceptual.answer: Clearer data flow; required for use in lambdas in many cases.

codeBased.question: Private field + public getter only.
codeBased.answer: private final String name; public String getName(){return name;}

codeBased.question: Builder pattern sketch.
codeBased.answer: static class B{ User build(){ validate(); return new User(...);}}

codeBased.question: Avoid setter for id after creation.
Use Instant instead of mutable Date.
Char array for password vs String.
codeBased.answer: char[] can be cleared; String stays in pool.

seniorScenario.question: DTOs mutable in API layer caused race—fix?
seniorScenario.answer: Immutable DTOs, separate command/query models, thread confinement.

seniorScenario.question: Hibernate entity “immutable” view—how?
seniorScenario.answer: Read-only queries, @Immutable annotation, separate projection records.

seniorScenario.question: PII in logs—encapsulation failure?
seniorScenario.answer: Redact in toString; structured logging with field filters.

seniorScenario.question: Breaking encapsulation via reflection in tests—OK?
seniorScenario.answer: Prefer package-visible test hooks or test doubles over production reflection.

seniorScenario.question: Large immutable object graphs—GC pressure?
seniorScenario.answer: Structural sharing (persistent collections) or careful batching—profile.

seniorScenario.question: Immutability and performance on hot path?
seniorScenario.answer: Object churn vs locking—measure; sometimes mutable buffers win in tight loops.

wrongAnswers: Encapsulation means all fields must be public for frameworks.
final class means all fields are automatically immutable.
Returning internal ArrayList reference is safe if you trust callers.
Records are deeply immutable for all component types.
StringBuilder should be used for all string fields in entities.
Immutability eliminates the need for synchronization always.

sections.content:

|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define Encapsulation and Immutability in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 2 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: Static nested class: tied to outer type but no implicit reference to outer instance.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: Inner class: holds reference to outer; can call outer instance methods.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: Local class inside method: captures effectively final variables from enclosing scope.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 13
sections.description: Visual model for Nested and Inner Classes

Ignoring trade-offs while choosing approach.

sections.solution:
// Day 13 Nested and Inner Classes - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is a static nested class?
conceptual.answer: Nested type not tied to outer instance; can be instantiated without outer instance.

conceptual.question: What is an inner class?
conceptual.answer: Non-static nested class holds implicit reference to outer instance.

conceptual.question: What is a local class?
conceptual.answer: Class declared inside a method; can access effectively final locals.

conceptual.question: What is an anonymous class?
conceptual.answer: Inline subclass of class or implementation of interface at instantiation site.

conceptual.question: Why use nested classes?
conceptual.answer: Logical grouping, encapsulation of helper types, access to outer private members.

conceptual.question: Memory leak risk with inner classes?
conceptual.answer: Long-lived inner holds outer reference—avoid if outer is heavy.

conceptual.question: Can static nested class access outer instance fields?
conceptual.answer: Only through an outer reference; not implicitly.

conceptual.question: What is effectively final?
conceptual.answer: Local variable not reassigned after init—can be captured by lambdas/inner classes.

conceptual.question: What is Enclosing instance for inner class?
conceptual.answer: Inner requires outer.this to construct from outside static context.

conceptual.question: What is private constructor with nested builder?
conceptual.answer: Outer controls creation via nested static builder class.

conceptual.question: Difference anonymous vs lambda?
conceptual.answer: Anonymous can implement multiple methods or stateful class; lambda is single SAM.

conceptual.question: Can nested interfaces exist?
conceptual.answer: Yes—often for namespacing callbacks.

conceptual.question: What is serialVersionUID with inner classes?
conceptual.answer: Serialization of non-static inner includes outer—careful with versioning.

conceptual.question: What is method-local enum (Java 16+)?
conceptual.answer: Enum inside method for small scoped constants.

conceptual.question: When prefer package-private top-level class over nested?
conceptual.answer: When type is reused widely—nested when truly tied to outer.

codeBased.question: Inner class instantiation.
codeBased.answer: Outer o = new Outer(); Outer.Inner i = o.new Inner();

codeBased.question: Local class capturing final int x.
codeBased.answer: int x=1; class L{ void f(){ System.out.println(x);} }

codeBased.question: Enum inside class.
codeBased.answer: static enum Kind { A,B } inside class.

codeBased.question: Access outer private from inner.
codeBased.answer: class Outer{ private int x; class I{ int g(){return x;}}}

codeBased.question: Method references to inner instance method.

seniorScenario.question: Thread leaks: non-static inner Runnable in long-lived executor—issue?
seniorScenario.answer: Holds outer; use static nested or weak references pattern.

seniorScenario.question: Serialization of non-static inner—problem?
seniorScenario.answer: Serializes synthetic outer reference—often not what you want.

seniorScenario.question: Testing private inner—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: Test via outer public API or package-visible test hook.

seniorScenario.question: Android/embedded: inner classes and dex size?
seniorScenario.answer: Many anonymous classes inflate size; lambdas may use invokedynamic.

seniorScenario.question: Code review: 5-level deep anonymous listeners—fix?
seniorScenario.answer: Named classes or lambdas with clear lifecycle.

seniorScenario.question: Access control: inner in same compilation unit accessing private outer—OK?
seniorScenario.answer: Yes, compiler generates synthetic accessors.

wrongAnswers: Inner classes can be instantiated without an outer instance.
static nested classes have an implicit reference to the outer object.
Anonymous classes can implement two unrelated interfaces at once.
Local classes can access non-final locals that are reassigned.
Nested classes are always static in Java.
Lambdas always allocate fewer objects than anonymous classes.

sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define Nested and Inner Classes in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |

### Day 14 Topic: Java Collections Framework
This lesson belongs to Phase 2 (OOP principles and core Java APIs) and targets Beginner level expectations.

### Core Concept
Start by defining Java Collections Framework in precise terms, then connect it to runtime behavior and design impact.

### Internal Understanding
For this topic, interviewers expect more than API recall. They look for:
- correct mental model
- edge-case awareness
- clean implementation choices
- trade-off reasoning

### Practical Implementation Approach
1. Build a baseline solution that is correct and readable.
2. Add validation for boundary, null/empty, or failure scenarios.
3. Measure or reason about time/space or operational cost.
4. Refactor into maintainable units.

### Interview Structure (recommended)
- What is it?
- Why is it needed?
- One practical Java example
- One common pitfall
- One better alternative (if constraints change)

### Production Perspective
In real systems, correctness alone is not enough. Java Collections Framework should also support observability, safe changes, and team readability.

### Revision Checklist

- Can you code it from memory?
- Can you test one edge case quickly?

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: ArrayList: dynamic-size List backed by array; add/get/size.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: HashMap: O(1) average lookups by key — count word frequencies.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: TreeMap: sorted keys; LinkedHashMap: insertion order — choose map by iteration needs.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 14
sections.description: Visual model for Java Collections Framework

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Treating Java Collections Framework as memorization instead of implementation practice.
Skipping edge-case tests and validating only happy path.
Using unclear naming and tightly coupled method logic.
Giving interview answers without concrete examples.
Ignoring trade-offs while choosing approach.

sections.title: Exercise - Practice Java Collections Framework
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution for **Java Collections Framework** with practical constraints.

Requirements:
1. Handle at least 5 test inputs.
2. Include one explicit edge/failure case.
3. Print deterministic output for verification.
4. Add short comments for decision points.
5. Keep the solution interview-readable and maintainable.
hints: Start with correctness first, optimize later.
Add one guard clause for invalid input.
Refactor method names before final answer.
sections.solution:
// Day 14 Java Collections Framework - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is the Collections Framework?
conceptual.answer: Unified interfaces (List, Set, Map) and implementations (ArrayList, HashMap, etc.).

conceptual.question: Difference between List and Set?
conceptual.answer: List ordered, allows duplicates; Set no duplicates (equals/hashCode contract).

conceptual.question: Difference between Map and Collection?
conceptual.answer: Map is key-value; not a Collection though part of collections package.

conceptual.question: What is ArrayList vs LinkedList complexity?
conceptual.answer: ArrayList random access O(1); LinkedList poor random access, good head/tail ops.

conceptual.question: What is HashMap load factor?
conceptual.answer: Default 0.75—balance between space and rehash frequency.

conceptual.question: What is TreeMap ordering?
conceptual.answer: Red-black tree sorted by key—O(log n) ops.

conceptual.question: What is LinkedHashMap iteration order?
conceptual.answer: Insertion order (or access-order for LRU mode).

conceptual.question: What is ConcurrentHashMap?
conceptual.answer: Thread-safe Map with segmented locking / CAS—prefer over synchronized HashMap.

conceptual.question: What is fail-fast iterator?
conceptual.answer: Throws ConcurrentModificationException on structural change during iteration (except iterator’s own remove).

conceptual.question: What is Collections.unmodifiableList?
conceptual.answer: Wrapper preventing modification through that reference—underlying may still change.

conceptual.question: What is equals/hashCode contract for HashMap keys?
conceptual.answer: Equal keys must have same hash; unequal keys should distribute hashes.

conceptual.question: Why avoid mutable keys in HashMap?
conceptual.answer: If key mutates after insert, lookup breaks—undefined behavior.

conceptual.question: What is Queue interface?
conceptual.answer: FIFO typically—offer/poll; Deque adds stack operations.

conceptual.question: What is PriorityQueue?
conceptual.answer: Heap-based—poll returns smallest according to comparator.

conceptual.question: What is immutable collections factories (Java 9+)?
conceptual.answer: List.of, Set.of, Map.of create unmodifiable structures.

codeBased.question: Convert array to list.
codeBased.answer: Arrays.asList(arr) fixed-size; new ArrayList<>(...) for mutable.

seniorScenario.question: HashMap infinite loop on bad hashCode—symptom?
seniorScenario.answer: CPU spin; ensure symmetric, well-distributed hash; security risk for user keys.

seniorScenario.question: Large HashMap resize pause—mitigation?
seniorScenario.answer: Pre-size capacity, G1 GC tuning, or off-heap structures if appropriate.

seniorScenario.question: Memory leak in static Map cache—fix?
seniorScenario.answer: Bounded cache, weak keys if appropriate, eviction policy.

seniorScenario.question: Concurrent modification in parallel streams—safe?
seniorScenario.answer: Source must be concurrent or side-effect free; structural change unsafe.

seniorScenario.question: Choosing List impl for workload—process?
seniorScenario.answer: Access pattern, size, memory, thread safety—benchmark.

seniorScenario.question: Deserialization gadget chains via collections—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Avoid deserializing untrusted data; use allowlists.

wrongAnswers: HashMap guarantees iteration order.
Vector is preferred over ArrayList for all new code.
Hashtable and HashMap are identical.
You can safely use mutable object as HashMap key if you are careful.
Collections.synchronizedList makes iterator thread-safe.
Map extends Collection in Java.

sections.solution:
// Day 15 Iterators and Enhanced For Loop - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is Iterator interface?
conceptual.answer: hasNext, next, remove—uniform traversal for collections.

conceptual.question: Difference iterator vs enhanced for?
conceptual.answer: for-each uses iterator under the hood; iterator allows remove during iteration.

conceptual.question: What is ListIterator?
conceptual.answer: Bidirectional iteration for List; can add/set at cursor.

conceptual.question: What is Iterable?
conceptual.answer: Classes implementing iterator() can be used in for-each.

conceptual.question: Why ConcurrentModificationException?
conceptual.answer: Structural modification while iterating with fail-fast iterator without using iterator.remove.

conceptual.question: What is weakly consistent iterator?
conceptual.answer: Concurrent collections may not throw CME; reflects best-effort view.

conceptual.question: What is Spliterator (Java 8+)?
conceptual.answer: Splitting iterator for parallel streams—supports trySplit.

conceptual.question: Can you remove with for-each?
conceptual.answer: No—use iterator.remove or collect to new structure.

conceptual.question: What is Enumeration legacy?
conceptual.answer: Older interface (Vector)—prefer Iterator.

conceptual.question: What is fail-safe iterator cost?
conceptual.answer: Copy snapshot—memory overhead for copy-on-write structures.

conceptual.question: What is cursor position in ListIterator?
conceptual.answer: Between elements; next() moves forward; previous() backward.

conceptual.question: What is modCount?
conceptual.answer: Internal counter tracking modifications for fail-fast behavior.

conceptual.question: Why stream() vs iterator manually?
conceptual.answer: Streams add declarative pipelines; iterator lower-level control.

conceptual.question: What is forEachRemaining on Iterator?
conceptual.answer: Consumes remaining elements with one lambda.

conceptual.question: What is immutable iteration?
conceptual.answer: Iterator over immutable snapshot—no CME because source doesn’t change.

codeBased.question: Avoid CME: use iterator not enhanced for when removing.
Iterable custom class.
codeBased.answer: class R implements Iterable<Integer>{ public Iterator<Integer> iterator(){...}}

codeBased.question: ConcurrentHashMap keySet iterator weak consistency.
codeBased.answer: No CME; may reflect partial updates.

seniorScenario.question: Big list processing OOM—iterator vs stream?
seniorScenario.answer: Use streaming from file or DB cursor instead of loading all.

seniorScenario.question: Parallel stream on ArrayList while another thread mutates—safe?
seniorScenario.answer: Unsafe—structural modification not supported; synchronize or use concurrent sources.

seniorScenario.question: Custom Spliterator for domain chunking—when?
seniorScenario.answer: Parallel pipelines over specialized data sources—correct trySplit crucial.

seniorScenario.question: Iterator contract violation in library—symptom?
seniorScenario.answer: Infinite loop or NPE; add tests for hasNext/next pairing.

seniorScenario.question: Legacy Enumeration in old API—migrate?
seniorScenario.answer: Adapter to Iterator; deprecate gradually.

seniorScenario.question: forEach on concurrent collection during update—behavior?
seniorScenario.answer: Depends on iterator type; document weak consistency to users.

wrongAnswers: Enhanced for-loop supports remove() on the collection directly.
Iterator.remove() works during enhanced for-each.
All iterators throw ConcurrentModificationException on any concurrent update.
ListIterator can traverse any Collection type.
Spliterator is only used for sequential streams.
modCount is part of public Iterator API.

sections.solution:
// Day 16 Comparable and Comparator - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is Comparable?
conceptual.answer: Interface for natural ordering: compareTo returns negative/zero/positive.

conceptual.question: What is Comparator?
conceptual.answer: External comparison strategy; can define multiple orderings for same type.

conceptual.question: What is natural ordering contract?
conceptual.answer: compareTo must be reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive, consistent with equals ideally.

conceptual.question: What is Comparator.comparing?
conceptual.answer: Build comparator from key extractors and method references.

conceptual.question: What is nullsFirst/nullsLast?
conceptual.answer: Comparator helpers to place nulls deterministically.

conceptual.question: Difference compareTo vs equals inconsistency?
conceptual.answer: TreeSet uses compareTo; if inconsistent with equals, Set contract breaks weirdly.

conceptual.question: What is stable sort?
conceptual.answer: TimSort preserves relative order of equal elements—important for multi-key sorts.

conceptual.question: What is reversed() on Comparator?
conceptual.answer: Flips ascending/descending order.

conceptual.question: What is thenComparing?
conceptual.answer: Lexicographic multi-field comparison.

conceptual.question: Why primitives use Integer.compare in compareTo?
conceptual.answer: Avoid overflow bugs from naive a-b subtraction.

conceptual.question: What is Arrays.sort for objects?
conceptual.answer: Requires Comparator or Comparable; uses TimSort.

conceptual.question: What is Collator for strings?
conceptual.answer: Locale-aware String comparison beyond Unicode code points.

conceptual.question: What is partial order vs total order?
conceptual.answer: Some pairs may be incomparable—rare in Comparable; Comparator can throw.

conceptual.question: What is priority queue ordering?
conceptual.answer: Head is least according to comparator or natural order.

conceptual.question: What is equals/hashCode with SortedSet?
conceptual.answer: Elements must compare consistently; duplicate “equal” elements not allowed in Set.

codeBased.question: class Person implements Comparable<Person> by name.
codeBased.answer: public int compareTo(Person o){ return name.compareTo(o.name);}

seniorScenario.question: Sorting user-visible list—wrong order in Turkish locale?
seniorScenario.answer: Use Collator.getInstance(locale) not String.compareTo.

seniorScenario.question: Database ORDER BY differs from Java sort—bug?
seniorScenario.answer: Normalize collation or sort in DB consistently with app.

seniorScenario.question: Comparator as hot path—micro-optimize?
seniorScenario.answer: Avoid boxing, cache comparators, measure before tricks.

seniorScenario.question: equals/compareTo mismatch in entity used in TreeSet—symptom?
seniorScenario.answer: Duplicates or “missing” elements—align contracts.

seniorScenario.question: Sorting JSON keys for canonical signatures—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: Recursive sort keys, defined byte encoding—security for crypto.

seniorScenario.question: Breaking change: Comparable implementation change—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: Persisted sorted sets may reorder—migration plan.

wrongAnswers: compareTo should return boolean.
Comparator and Comparable are the same interface.
You can use a-b for all int comparisons in compareTo safely.
TreeSet uses equals to decide membership only.
nulls are sorted first by default in all JDK sorts.
Arrays.sort on primitives uses the same TimSort as objects.

|---|---|---|---|

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is java.io vs java.nio?
conceptual.answer: Classic streams vs NIO buffers/channels—NIO.2 (java.nio.file) for modern file APIs.

conceptual.question: What is Path vs File?
conceptual.answer: Path (NIO.2) preferred; File has legacy issues.

conceptual.question: What is Files.readAllLines?
conceptual.answer: Convenience for small text files—loads entire file into memory.

conceptual.question: What is try-with-resources for files?
conceptual.answer: Ensures streams closed—Files.newBufferedReader etc.

conceptual.question: What is Charset/StandardCharsets?
conceptual.answer: Specify encoding explicitly—avoid platform default surprises.

conceptual.question: What is WatchService?
conceptual.answer: Watch directory for changes—event-driven file monitoring.

conceptual.question: What is FileVisitor for walkFileTree?
conceptual.answer: Walk directory tree with pre/post directory hooks.

conceptual.question: Difference InputStream vs Reader?
conceptual.answer: Bytes vs characters; Reader uses charset decoding.

conceptual.question: What is memory-mapped files?
conceptual.answer: MapFileChannel to ByteBuffer for large file access patterns.

conceptual.question: What is IOException hierarchy?
conceptual.answer: Many checked IO exceptions—must handle or declare.

conceptual.question: What is Files.createTempFile?
conceptual.answer: Creates temp file with proper attributes—delete on exit carefully.

conceptual.question: What is symbolic link handling?
conceptual.answer: Files.readSymbolicLink, FOLLOW_LINKS option in walk.

conceptual.question: What is FileSystems.getDefault?
conceptual.answer: Access to default provider; can get FileSystem for ZIP/JAR.

conceptual.question: Why avoid File.separator concatenation strings?
conceptual.answer: Use Path.resolve for cross-platform correctness.

conceptual.question: What is buffering for performance?
conceptual.answer: BufferedReader/BufferedWriter reduce system calls.

codeBased.question: Files.readString(Path.of("a.txt"))
codeBased.answer: Java 11+ read entire file as String with charset.

seniorScenario.question: Log rotation fills disk—ops?
seniorScenario.answer: External logrotate, quotas, monitoring; app should not swallow IOException on write.

seniorScenario.question: Reading huge file with readAllBytes—OOM?
seniorScenario.answer: Stream with lines or channels; chunk processing.

seniorScenario.question: File locking between processes—approach?
seniorScenario.answer: FileLock on FileChannel; handle overlap and NFS caveats.

seniorScenario.question: Sensitive data in temp files—risk?
seniorScenario.answer: SecureDirectoryStream, restrictive permissions, delete in finally.

seniorScenario.question: Watching config directory—thundering herd?
seniorScenario.answer: Debounce events, reload idempotently.

seniorScenario.question: Windows vs Linux path case sensitivity—bug?
seniorScenario.answer: Test on both; normalize paths for keys.

wrongAnswers: Files.readAllLines is always the best choice for any file size.
Reader reads raw bytes without decoding.
Path and File are unrelated—you cannot convert between them.
IOException is unchecked so you can ignore it.
Default charset is always UTF-8 on every JVM.
Files.deleteIfExists never fails.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define File I/O and NIO.2 in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 2 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |

days.title: Enums and Records
learningObjectives: Explain Enums and Records with clear interview-ready wording
Implement Enums and Records using compilable Java examples for Day 18
Identify and prevent common bugs related to Enums and Records
Discuss design and performance trade-offs for Enums and Records
Solve at least one realistic exercise based on Enums and Records
Answer conceptual, coding, and scenario interview questions confidently

sections.title: Why Enums and Records matters
sections.content:
Enums and Records is a high-impact topic for Day 18. It directly supports OOP principles and core Java APIs and appears in interviews as a depth-check area.

Why this matters in practice:
- Teams rely on this concept for clean implementation decisions.
- Mistakes here create maintainability and production-risk issues.
- Strong understanding improves both coding rounds and system discussions.

Learning strategy for this day:
1. Define the concept clearly in one line.
2. Implement one minimal Java example.
3. Validate at least one edge/failure case.
4. Explain one trade-off (simplicity vs flexibility, speed vs safety, etc.).
5. Rephrase your answer as if explaining to an interviewer.

If you can explain Enums and Records clearly and implement it without copy-paste, you are interview-ready for this unit.

sections.title: Theory and Internals - Enums and Records
sections.content:
### Day 18 Topic: Enums and Records
This lesson belongs to Phase 2 (OOP principles and core Java APIs) and targets Beginner level expectations.

### Core Concept
Start by defining Enums and Records in precise terms, then connect it to runtime behavior and design impact.

### Internal Understanding
For this topic, interviewers expect more than API recall. They look for:
- correct mental model
- edge-case awareness
- clean implementation choices
- trade-off reasoning

### Practical Implementation Approach
1. Build a baseline solution that is correct and readable.
2. Add validation for boundary, null/empty, or failure scenarios.
3. Measure or reason about time/space or operational cost.
4. Refactor into maintainable units.

### Interview Structure (recommended)
- What is it?
- Why is it needed?
- One practical Java example
- One common pitfall
- One better alternative (if constraints change)

### Production Perspective
In real systems, correctness alone is not enough. Enums and Records should also support observability, safe changes, and team readability.

### Revision Checklist
- Can you explain this without notes?
- Can you code it from memory?
- Can you test one edge case quickly?
- Can you justify your approach in 30-60 seconds?

sections.title: Basic Walkthrough
sections.description: enum: fixed set of constants with type safety vs magic strings.

sections.title: Intermediate Implementation
sections.description: enum with fields/methods: attach metadata (HTTP status code) to each constant.

sections.title: Advanced Production Pattern
sections.description: record: immutable data carrier; equals/hashCode/toString for free.

sections.title: Architecture View - Day 18
sections.description: Visual model for Enums and Records

sections.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Treating Enums and Records as memorization instead of implementation practice.
Skipping edge-case tests and validating only happy path.
Using unclear naming and tightly coupled method logic.
Giving interview answers without concrete examples.
Ignoring trade-offs while choosing approach.

sections.title: Exercise - Practice Enums and Records
sections.problem:
Implement a Java solution for **Enums and Records** with practical constraints.

Requirements:
1. Handle at least 5 test inputs.
2. Include one explicit edge/failure case.
3. Print deterministic output for verification.
4. Add short comments for decision points.
5. Keep the solution interview-readable and maintainable.
hints: Start with correctness first, optimize later.
Add one guard clause for invalid input.
Refactor method names before final answer.
sections.solution:
// Day 18 Enums and Records - reference solution intentionally omitted in prompt.
// Reuse the "Advanced Production Pattern" code block above as base and add edge-case handling.

sections.title: Interview Drill

conceptual.question: What is an enum in Java?
conceptual.answer: Type-safe fixed set of constants; full class with optional fields/methods.

conceptual.question: Why prefer enum over string constants?
conceptual.answer: Compiler-checked exhaustiveness in switch; no invalid values.

conceptual.question: Can enum have constructors?
conceptual.answer: Yes—private (implicitly) constructors initialize constant-specific state.

conceptual.question: What is enum ordinal?
conceptual.answer: Position in declaration—avoid relying on it for business logic (fragile to reorder).

conceptual.question: What is EnumSet?
conceptual.answer: Compact bit-vector set of enum elements—very efficient.

conceptual.question: What is EnumMap?
conceptual.answer: Array-backed map keyed by enum—faster than HashMap for enum keys.

conceptual.question: What is Java record?
conceptual.answer: Immutable data carrier with generated accessors, equals, hashCode, toString.

conceptual.question: What is compact constructor in record?
conceptual.answer: Validation/normalization without repeating field list.

conceptual.question: Can record implement interfaces?
conceptual.answer: Yes—cannot extend classes (already extends Record implicitly).

conceptual.question: What is sealed class/interface?
conceptual.answer: Restricts subclasses—exhaustive pattern matching with compiler.

conceptual.question: Difference enum singleton vs class singleton?
conceptual.answer: Enum singleton is serialization-safe and thread-safe by JVM.

conceptual.question: What is switch on enum exhaustiveness?
conceptual.answer: Compiler warns if cases missing (depending on settings).

conceptual.question: What is record pattern (Java 21+)?
conceptual.answer: Deconstruct record in instanceof/switch patterns.

conceptual.question: Why avoid enum with behavior in domain—criticism?
conceptual.answer: Can become God enum; sometimes strategy pattern clearer.

conceptual.question: What is serialization of enum?
conceptual.answer: Special handling by name—stable across versions if constants stable.

codeBased.question: record User(String email){ compact constructor { if(email==null) throw ...}}
sealed interface Expr permits Add, Num

seniorScenario.question: API returns new enum constant—client switch breaks?
seniorScenario.answer: Use default branch or exhaustive handling; communicate API versioning.

seniorScenario.question: JPA enum ordinal vs string storage—trade-off?
seniorScenario.answer: String readable in DB; ordinal breaks on reorder—prefer STRING or explicit code.

seniorScenario.question: Record in public API—binary compatibility when adding field?
seniorScenario.answer: Adding component is breaking change—use versioning or wrapper.

seniorScenario.question: Large EnumSet in hot path—memory?
seniorScenario.answer: Very compact; still profile if millions of sets.

seniorScenario.question: Reflection on records—framework support?
seniorScenario.answer: Frameworks use accessor names; annotation processors adapt.

seniorScenario.question: Sealed classes and Spring proxies—issue?
seniorScenario.answer: CGLIB subclasses may conflict with sealed permits—document constraints.

wrongAnswers: enum constants can be created at runtime with new.
record can extend another class besides java.lang.Record.
ordinal() is stable even if you reorder enum constants.
EnumMap allows null keys like HashMap.
sealed classes allow unlimited subclasses in any package.
switch on enum requires default branch always.

sections.title: Cheatsheet
sections.content:
| Area | Rule | Interview Tip | Gotcha |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concept | Define Enums and Records in 1-2 lines | Start with purpose first | Vague definitions |
| Code | Write minimal compilable example | Explain expected output | Hidden edge-case bug |
| Validation | Test boundary + invalid input | Mention test strategy | Only happy-path tests |
| Trade-off | Compare 2 approaches | Link choice to constraints | "Depends" with no criteria |
| Phase Context | Align with phase 2 focus | Show progression mindset | Isolated-topic thinking |
